subtopic_detail: Anovulatory subfertility in polycystic ovary syndrome
stem: A 28-year-old presents for fertility advice after 14 months of unprotected intercourse without conception. She has oligomenorrhoea with cycles every 50 to 70 days and a body mass index of 29. Her partner has had a recent semen analysis showing normal count and motility. Pelvic ultrasound shows polycystic ovarian morphology bilaterally. Anti-Mullerian hormone is elevated. There is no tubal pathology on hysterosalpingogram.

data_table.Investigations: Day-3 FSH 5.5 IU/L, LH 9 IU/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, prolactin 320 mIU/L, fasting glucose 5.6, HbA1c 5.4%, total testosterone 2.6 nmol/L, sex hormone binding globulin 28 nmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention for ovulation induction?

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill for 3 months followed by clomiphene citrate 50 mg daily for days 2 to 6 of the next withdrawal bleed
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception suppresses ovulation and is not used to induce conception. (Source: RANZCOG: PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Subcutaneous recombinant follicle stimulating hormone with daily ultrasound monitoring as first-line ovulation induction
options.rationale: Gonadotrophin injections require intensive monitoring and are reserved for those failing oral ovulation induction. (Source: RANZCOG: PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Metformin 500 mg twice daily monotherapy with no other ovulation induction agent for the next 6 months
options.rationale: Metformin alone has lower live-birth rates than letrozole; it is adjunctive rather than first-line for anovulatory subfertility. (Source: RANZCOG: PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling as the initial intervention to restore monthly ovulation without the need for medication
options.rationale: Ovarian drilling is second-line surgery reserved for women who do not ovulate on oral therapy or those needing laparoscopy for another reason. (Source: RANZCOG: PCOS Guideline)

options.text: Letrozole 2.5 mg daily for days 2 to 6 of the cycle with mid-luteal progesterone confirmation of ovulation
options.rationale: Letrozole is now the recommended first-line ovulation induction agent in polycystic ovary syndrome with higher live-birth rates than clomiphene. (Source: RANZCOG: PCOS Guideline)

explanation.summary: Polycystic ovary syndrome causes anovulatory subfertility through chronic anovulation. International evidence-based guidance now recommends letrozole as the first-line ovulation induction agent because of higher cumulative live-birth rates compared with clomiphene citrate. Clomiphene remains an alternative if letrozole is contraindicated or unavailable. Metformin is adjunctive for insulin resistance and may improve ovulation rates when combined with letrozole, particularly in higher body mass index. Lifestyle weight loss of 5-10% restores ovulation in many women and should accompany pharmacological induction.
key_points: Letrozole is first-line ovulation induction in PCOS
Lifestyle and 5-10% weight loss adjuncts restore ovulation in many women
Gonadotrophins or ovarian drilling are second-line
explanation.pearls: Letrozole carries no increased risk of congenital anomaly compared with clomiphene despite earlier safety concerns from short courses.

subtopic_detail: Anti-D immunoglobulin after antepartum sensitising event
stem: A 26-year-old in her first pregnancy at 21 weeks presents 3 hours after a moderate fall down two stairs at home with right-sided abdominal contact with the bannister. She is RhD negative with no preformed anti-D antibodies on her booking screen. Her foetal movements are still felt. Routine 28-week prophylaxis has not yet been administered. The on-call obstetric registrar has reviewed her and found no antepartum haemorrhage.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, RR 18, T 36.8, BP 116/72, foetal heart rate 148 by handheld Doppler
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no uterine tenderness, no contractions on tocography, mild bruising over the right flank
data_table.Investigations: Speculum shows no bleeding; Kleihauer-Betke 0.5 mL foetal cells per 5 mL maternal blood; group and antibody screen confirms RhD negative with no anti-D

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step for RhD alloimmunisation prevention?

options.text: Administer intramuscular anti-D 250 IU now and avoid routine 28-week and 34-week prophylaxis because of the recent event dose given today
options.rationale: The 250 IU dose is for sensitising events before 12 weeks; from 13 weeks the dose is 625 IU, and routine antenatal prophylaxis is still offered at 28 and 34 weeks. (Source: NBA: Anti-D Use Guideline)

options.text: Administer intramuscular anti-D 625 IU within 72 hours of the event and continue routine 28-week and 34-week prophylaxis as scheduled
options.rationale: For sensitising events at or after 13 weeks the dose is 625 IU within 72 hours; routine prophylaxis continues unless given within the preceding 3 weeks. (Source: NBA: Anti-D Use Guideline)

options.text: Administer intravenous anti-D 1500 IU now and titrate with daily Kleihauer-Betke until below 0.1 mL foetal cells per 5 mL maternal blood
options.rationale: Standard intramuscular dosing covers the Kleihauer-Betke estimate here; daily Kleihauer titration is not standard and intravenous use is restricted to specific clotting risk scenarios. (Source: NBA: Anti-D Use Guideline)

options.text: Defer anti-D and arrange repeat Kleihauer-Betke at 48 hours, treating only if the level rises above 1 mL foetal cells per 5 mL maternal blood
options.rationale: Anti-D after a sensitising event is time-critical; delay beyond 72 hours reduces effectiveness, with sensitisation rates rising sharply thereafter. (Source: NBA: Anti-D Use Guideline)

options.text: Defer anti-D because the maternal-foetal haemorrhage is less than 4 mL, and reassess only if she develops new bleeding or contractions overnight
options.rationale: Any clinically significant sensitising event after 13 weeks warrants 625 IU within 72 hours regardless of Kleihauer estimate within standard volume ranges. (Source: NBA: Anti-D Use Guideline)

explanation.summary: RhD-negative women without preformed anti-D require anti-D prophylaxis after sensitising events including abdominal trauma in pregnancy. Dosing is 250 IU before 13 weeks and 625 IU at or after 13 weeks, given within 72 hours of the event. Routine antenatal prophylaxis is still offered at 28 and 34 weeks unless a 625 IU dose was given within the preceding 3 weeks. Kleihauer-Betke is used to detect feto-maternal haemorrhage exceeding 6 mL of foetal cells, which requires additional anti-D.
key_points: 250 IU before 13 weeks, 625 IU from 13 weeks for sensitising events
Give anti-D within 72 hours of any sensitising event
Continue routine 28-week and 34-week prophylaxis unless within 3 weeks of an event dose
explanation.pearls: Falls in pregnancy without bleeding still count as sensitising events from 20 weeks; treat by default and use Kleihauer to detect larger feto-maternal haemorrhage.

subtopic_detail: Acute ovarian torsion with intermittent pain
stem: A 22-year-old presents with 5 hours of sudden severe right iliac fossa pain that began during exercise. The pain is colicky with two episodes of severe vomiting. She uses condoms only and her last menstrual period was 12 days ago. There is no fever and no abnormal vaginal discharge. There is no urinary frequency or dysuria.

data_table.Examination: Right iliac fossa tenderness with mild guarding, no rebound, no rigidity; bimanual examination reveals tender right adnexal mass approximately 6 cm
data_table.Investigations: Urinary pregnancy test negative, urinalysis bland, FBC WCC 12 with neutrophilia; CRP 22; transabdominal ultrasound shows a 6 cm complex right adnexal cyst with markedly reduced colour Doppler flow within the ovary

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia, with repeat ultrasound in 6 hours if pain persists or worsens during overnight nursing review
options.rationale: Delaying intervention in suspected ovarian torsion risks irreversible ischaemic loss of ovarian tissue. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Pelvic Pain)

options.text: Commence intravenous ceftriaxone plus metronidazole empirically for tubo-ovarian abscess and reassess after 24 hours of antibiotics
options.rationale: Tubo-ovarian abscess typically has fever, leucocytosis, and inflammatory adnexal fluid; ultrasound here shows compromised flow rather than abscess. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: pelvic inflammatory disease)

options.text: Arrange an outpatient magnetic resonance imaging scan within 1 week and gynaecology clinic appointment within 2 weeks for the adnexal mass
options.rationale: Outpatient pathway is inappropriate when ultrasound and clinical features support torsion; this risks ovarian loss within hours. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Pelvic Pain)

options.text: Arrange urgent gynaecology referral for same-day diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian conservation if viable
options.rationale: Suspected ovarian torsion is a surgical diagnosis warranting same-day laparoscopy; detorsion with ovarian conservation is preferred over oophorectomy. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Pelvic Pain)

options.text: Trial transvaginal ultrasound-guided cyst aspiration in the emergency department as an alternative to laparoscopy
options.rationale: Cyst aspiration does not address torsion and may cause cyst rupture and chemical peritonitis. (Source: RANZCOG: Acute Pelvic Pain)

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency requiring same-day laparoscopy. The classic triad of sudden pelvic pain, palpable mass, and reduced or absent ovarian Doppler flow has high specificity but absent flow is not required for diagnosis as venous compromise often precedes arterial loss. Detorsion with preservation of the ovary is preferred over oophorectomy even when the ovary appears dusky, because viable cortex often recovers. Cyst aspiration is not therapeutic and risks chemical peritonitis.
key_points: Acute pelvic pain with adnexal mass and reduced Doppler flow needs same-day laparoscopy
Detorsion with ovarian conservation is preferred over oophorectomy
Doppler flow can be preserved early due to dual ovarian blood supply
explanation.pearls: Even a black ischaemic-appearing ovary often recovers function after detorsion; never assume non-viability based on intraoperative colour alone.

subtopic_detail: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes at 34 weeks gestation
stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 34+0 weeks presents with 4 hours of clear fluid leakage confirmed on sterile speculum. She has no contractions, no fever, and is GBS negative on this pregnancy's screening. The foetus is in cephalic presentation with reassuring cardiotocography. She has had no antenatal corticosteroids in this pregnancy. She would like to know about timing of delivery.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 86, RR 16, T 36.7, BP 118/72, foetal heart rate baseline 140 with normal variability
data_table.Examination: Sterile speculum confirms pooling clear amniotic fluid; cervix appears 2 cm dilated with no chorioamnionitis features; uterus non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Maternal FBC WCC 9.6, CRP 6, midstream urine bland; ultrasound confirms cephalic, normal Doppler, estimated foetal weight 2.3 kg, amniotic fluid index 7

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Admit for expectant management with antenatal corticosteroids, infection surveillance, and planned delivery at 37+0 weeks with prophylactic antibiotics if labour starts first
options.rationale: From 24 to 36+6 weeks expectant management with steroids and infection surveillance is recommended; planned delivery by 37+0 weeks balances respiratory and infection risks. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Immediate caesarean delivery today with administration of betamethasone in theatre after delivery to mitigate neonatal respiratory distress syndrome
options.rationale: Steroids are given before birth to be effective; immediate caesarean without indication does not improve outcomes at this gestation. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal Corticosteroids)

options.text: Discharge home with 7 days of oral erythromycin and weekly outpatient review of foetal welfare and maternal inflammatory markers in clinic
options.rationale: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes requires inpatient surveillance; outpatient management is not standard given chorioamnionitis risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

options.text: Induce labour immediately with vaginal misoprostol and continue magnesium sulfate infusion for neuroprotection throughout induction
options.rationale: Magnesium neuroprotection is for delivery under 30 weeks; immediate induction at 34 weeks without other indication carries higher respiratory morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: Magnesium Sulfate for Neuroprotection)

options.text: Commence tocolysis with nifedipine and inpatient bed rest until 37 weeks gestation without antibiotics, steroids, or further inflammatory markers
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not indicated without active preterm labour; antibiotics and steroids are routine adjuncts in preterm prelabour rupture of membranes. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

explanation.summary: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes from 24 to 36+6 weeks is managed expectantly with antenatal corticosteroids, regular infection surveillance, and prophylactic antibiotics if labour starts or chorioamnionitis is suspected. Australian guidance now favours planned delivery at 37+0 weeks rather than immediate delivery at 34 weeks because respiratory morbidity outweighs neonatal infection risk in this range. Inpatient surveillance with serial maternal observations and fetal monitoring is standard. Magnesium neuroprotection applies for births before 30 weeks.
key_points: Expectant management with steroids and surveillance from 24 to 36+6 weeks
Plan delivery at 37+0 weeks in stable cases
Tocolysis is not indicated without active preterm labour
explanation.pearls: Group B Streptococcus prophylaxis is required in labour regardless of negative screen if rupture exceeds 18 hours; protocols vary by health service.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Preterm Prelabour Rupture of Membranes

subtopic_detail: Suspected placenta accreta spectrum at 28 weeks
stem: A 34-year-old G4P3 has had three previous caesarean deliveries. Her morphology ultrasound at 22 weeks reported a low-lying anterior placenta covering the previous uterine scar. A repeat scan at 28 weeks now reports loss of the clear retroplacental space, irregular intraplacental lacunae with turbulent flow, and bridging vessels crossing the uterine bladder interface. She is asymptomatic with no antepartum haemorrhage. She lives 90 minutes from the regional referral hospital.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 84, RR 16, T 36.6, BP 116/74, foetal heart rate 142 with normal variability
data_table.Investigations: Targeted ultrasound concerning for placenta accreta spectrum; bloods: Hb 110, platelets 230, fibrinogen 4.2, group and antibody screen for blood bank notification

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate antenatal management plan?

options.text: Continue local antenatal care and plan elective lower segment caesarean section at 39 weeks at her local hospital with on-call gynaecology
options.rationale: Placenta accreta spectrum requires delivery at a tertiary centre with multidisciplinary expertise; local elective caesarean risks catastrophic haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Order magnetic resonance imaging of the pelvis today and only refer to a tertiary maternal-foetal medicine unit if posterior involvement is confirmed
options.rationale: Magnetic resonance imaging is adjunctive in posterior or equivocal cases but referral is based on clinical suspicion, not awaiting imaging. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Admit her at 32 weeks for daily ultrasound surveillance and plan vaginal delivery on the labour ward if cephalic at 37 weeks
options.rationale: Vaginal delivery is contraindicated in placenta accreta spectrum because of catastrophic haemorrhage risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Trial of conservative management with placenta in situ at delivery aiming for delayed expulsion and uterine preservation as the primary plan
options.rationale: Conservative leave-in-situ is selective; the default plan at multidisciplinary centres remains planned caesarean hysterectomy unless future fertility is a priority. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

options.text: Refer to a tertiary multidisciplinary placenta accreta service for steroids, blood bank planning, and elective caesarean hysterectomy at 35 to 36 weeks
options.rationale: Tertiary multidisciplinary care with planned 35 to 36 week elective caesarean hysterectomy under cell salvage and major haemorrhage protocols halves maternal morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta Accreta Spectrum)

explanation.summary: Placenta accreta spectrum is associated with prior caesarean and placenta praevia. Antenatal imaging features include loss of the retroplacental clear space, intraplacental lacunae, and bridging vessels at the uterus-bladder interface. Tertiary multidisciplinary care including blood bank, urology, interventional radiology, and intensive care reduces maternal morbidity. The default plan is elective lower-segment caesarean delivery with caesarean hysterectomy at 35 to 36+6 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids. Magnetic resonance imaging is adjunctive in posterior placentation or where ultrasound is equivocal.
key_points: Placenta accreta spectrum demands tertiary multidisciplinary care
Plan elective delivery at 35 to 36+6 weeks after antenatal corticosteroids
Caesarean hysterectomy is the default; conservative management is highly selective
explanation.pearls: Three or more previous caesareans with anterior praevia carries up to a 60% risk of placenta accreta spectrum; have a high pre-test probability.
